turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: In case number 17-333, Benisek v. Lamone, we are issuing a per curiam opinion. This case comes to us on direct appeal from the District Court for the District of Maryland. It concerns allegations by several Republican voters that in 2011 the Maryland General Assembly gerrymandered the lines of Maryland's Sixth Congressional District for the purpose of unseating an incumbent Republican Congressman. In May 2017, six years after the Sixth District was redrawn, the plaintiffs moved the District Court for a preliminary injunction barring use of the current congressional map. In order to allow time for the creation of a new map, the plaintiffs urged the District Court to enter a preliminary injunction by August 18, 2017. The District Court was not able to act within that deadline and it denied the plaintiff's motion after finding that they had failed to show a sufficient likelihood of success on the merits of their claim to warrant a preliminary injunction. The District Court also stayed its proceedings pending this Court's decision in Gill v. Whitford. The Court noted that it was concerned about measuring the legality and constitutionality of any redistricting plan in Maryland according to the proper legal standard and that it would be better equipped to make that determination after this Court issued a decision in Gill. The plaintiff's appeal to this Court and we now affirm the District Court's decision not to enter a preliminary injunction. A preliminary injunction is never awarded as of right and a District Court considering the issuance of such an injunction must consider not only the merits of a plaintiff's claim, but also whether the plaintiff is likely to suffer irreparable injury in the absence of an injunction, whether the balance of equities tips in the plaintiff's favor, and whether an injunction is in the public interest. In this case we think that even assuming the plaintiffs were likely to succeed on the merits of their gerrymandering claim, the balance of equities and the public interest tilted against their request for a preliminary injunction. That is due not only to the plaintiff's delay in seeking one, but also to the tight time constraints under which the District Court was operating, the risk of disrupting Maryland's election schedule, and the District Court's interest in staying the proceedings pending this Court's decision in Gill. In these particular circumstances we conclude that the District Court's decision denying a preliminary injunction cannot be regarded as an abuse of discretion. The per curiam is unanimous.
